title: Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999
chapters: SI 1999/3242 · In Force 29 December 1999 · Replaced MHSWR 1992 (SI 1992/2051) · Implements EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC · Made under HSWA 1974
desc: The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 are the principal subordinate regulations under HSWA 1974, implementing EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC. They impose detailed management duties including: risk assessment (reg.3), health and safety arrangements (reg.5), health surveillance (reg.6), appointment of competent persons (reg.7), procedures for serious and imminent danger (reg.8), information for employees (reg.10), cooperation and coordination (reg.11), capabilities and training (reg.13), employees' duties (reg.14), temporary workers (reg.15), new and expectant mothers risk assessment (reg.16), and young persons risk assessment (reg.19). Regulation 3 (risk assessment) is the foundational obligation — it drives the entire UK health and safety regulatory framework.

sections.title: Risk assessment
sections.summary: Every employer must make a suitable and sufficient assessment of the risks to employees and non-employees arising from the undertaking. Employers with 5+ employees must record the significant findings and any group of employees identified as being especially at risk. The assessment must be reviewed when there is reason to suspect it is no longer valid or there has been a significant change. The risk assessment is the foundation of UK H&S compliance — it drives all subsequent control measures.
sections.text:
(1) Every employer shall make a suitable and sufficient assessment of—
(a) the risks to the health and safety of his employees to which they are exposed whilst they are at work; and
(b) the risks to the health and safety of persons not in his employment arising out of or in connection with the conduct by him of his undertaking,
for the purpose of identifying the measures he needs to take to comply with the requirements and prohibitions imposed upon him by or under the relevant statutory provisions.

(6) Where the employer employs five or more employees, he shall record—
(a) the significant findings of the assessment; and
(b) any group of his employees identified by it as being especially at risk.

versions.description: Original MHSWR 1992 (SI 1992/2051) introduced the risk assessment obligation, implementing EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC.
Replaced by MHSWR 1999 (SI 1999/3242). Extended and clarified requirements. Added new/expectant mothers and young persons provisions.

leadingCases.name: Allison v London Underground Ltd [2008] ICR 636
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the obligation to assess psychosocial risks is part of the general risk assessment duty. Stress-related risks must be assessed alongside physical risks.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed psychosocial risks fall within scope of reg.3 risk assessment

refs: HSE Approved Code of Practice L21

sections.title: Health and safety assistance — competent persons
sections.summary: Every employer must appoint one or more competent persons to assist in undertaking the measures needed to comply with health and safety legislation. A competent person is someone who has sufficient training, experience, knowledge and other qualities to help the employer meet their obligations. Where possible, preference should be given to appointing someone from within the employer's own workforce rather than an external consultant.
sections.text:
(1) Every employer shall, subject to paragraphs (6) and (7), appoint one or more competent persons to assist him in undertaking the measures he needs to take to comply with the requirements and prohibitions imposed upon him by or under the relevant statutory provisions.

(5) A person shall be regarded as competent for the purposes of paragraphs (1) and (2) where he has sufficient training and experience or knowledge and other qualities to enable him properly to assist in undertaking the measures referred to in those paragraphs.

(8) Where there is a competent person in the employer's employment, that person shall be appointed for the purposes of paragraph (1) in preference to a competent person not in his employment.

versions.description: Original MHSWR 1992 reg.6 established the competent person requirement.
MHSWR 1999 reg.7 replaced the 1992 provision. Added the preference for in-house competent persons over external consultants (para.8).

leadingCases.name: R v Chargot Ltd [2008] UKHL 73
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords considered the employer's duty to ensure health and safety so far as reasonably practicable. The appointment of competent persons under reg.7 is part of the management system required to discharge the duty. The employer must demonstrate they have adequate arrangements including competent advice.
leadingCases.significance: Competent person appointment as part of the management system required for compliance.

refs: reg.3 MHSWR 1999 (risk assessment — competent person assists)

sections.title: Risk assessment in respect of new or expectant mothers
sections.summary: Where the persons working in an undertaking include women of childbearing age and the work could involve risk to the health and safety of a new or expectant mother or her baby from any processes, working conditions, or physical, biological or chemical agents, the employer must include in the reg.3 risk assessment an assessment of such risks. If the assessment reveals a risk, the employer must take action: alter working conditions or hours, offer suitable alternative work, or suspend on full pay. This obligation is critical for compliance with both H&S law and pregnancy/maternity discrimination law under EqA 2010 s.18.
sections.text:
(1) Where—
(a) the persons working in an undertaking include women of childbearing age; and
(b) the work is of a kind which could involve risk, by reason of her condition, to the health and safety of a new or expectant mother, or to that of her baby, from any processes or working conditions, or physical, biological or chemical agents,
the assessment required by regulation 3(1) shall also include an assessment of such risk.

(2) Where, in the case of an individual employee, the taking of any other action the employer is required to take under the relevant statutory provisions would not avoid the risk referred to in paragraph (1) the employer shall, if it is reasonable to do so, and would avoid such risks, alter her working conditions or hours of work.

(3) If it is not reasonable to alter the working conditions or hours of work, or if it would not avoid such risk, the employer shall, subject to section 67 of the 1996 Act, suspend her from work for so long as is necessary to avoid such risk.

versions.description: Original MHSWR 1992 first introduced new/expectant mothers risk assessment requirement.
MHSWR 1999 reg.16 replaced and expanded the 1992 provision. Clarified the three-stage response: alter working conditions/hours → offer suitable alternative → suspend on full pay.

leadingCases.name: Day v T Pickles Farms Ltd [1999] IRLR 217
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that failure to carry out a specific risk assessment for a pregnant employee is a breach of reg.16 and may amount to sex discrimination. The employer cannot simply rely on the general risk assessment — a specific assessment of risks to the new or expectant mother is required.
leadingCases.significance: Established that a specific pregnancy risk assessment is mandatory — the general assessment is not sufficient

pendingAmendments: Post-Brexit — MHSWR 1999 now assimilated law under REUL Act 2023
No ERA 2025 amendments to MHSWR directly
HSE ACOP L21 periodically reviewed — monitoring for updates

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 s.15(1), (2), (3)(a), (4)(a), (5)(b), (9) and s.82(3)(a)
parliamentaryOrigin.euDirective: Council Directive 89/391/EEC (Framework Directive on Safety and Health at Work)
parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for the Environment, Transport and the Regions
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The MHSWR 1999 replaced the MHSWR 1992 (SI 1992/2051) which first implemented the EU Framework Directive. The 1999 Regulations extended and clarified the management duties. Regulation 3 (risk assessment) is the foundational obligation in UK health and safety law — it drives all subsequent control measures, training, monitoring, and review. The Framework Directive required member states to establish comprehensive management systems for occupational health and safety. The MHSWR 1999 are the principal subordinate regulations under HSWA 1974.

precedingDocuments.title: EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC
precedingDocuments.significance: The governing EU Framework Directive establishing the obligation on employers to carry out risk assessment (Art.6(3)(a)), appoint competent persons, provide information and training, and establish emergency procedures. The MHSWR 1999 implement the Framework Directive in the UK.

precedingDocuments.title: MHSWR 1992 (SI 1992/2051)
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor regulations replaced by MHSWR 1999. First introduced the risk assessment obligation in UK law on 1 January 1993.

precedingDocuments.title: HSE Approved Code of Practice L21: Management of Health and Safety at Work
precedingDocuments.significance: The ACOP has special legal status under HSWA 1974 s.17 — failure to follow it is admissible in criminal proceedings and the employer must show they complied with the law in some other way.

leadingCases.name: Allison v London Underground [2008] ICR 636
leadingCases.heldText: Psychosocial risks (stress) must be assessed alongside physical risks under reg.3.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed stress risk assessment falls within reg.3 scope.

leadingCases.name: R v Chargot Ltd [2008] UKHL 73
leadingCases.heldText: The employer must demonstrate adequate management arrangements including competent advice to discharge the duty of reasonable practicability.
leadingCases.significance: Management system and competent person as part of the compliance framework.

leadingCases.name: Day v T Pickles Farms [1999] IRLR 217
leadingCases.heldText: Failure to carry out a specific pregnancy risk assessment under reg.16 may amount to sex discrimination.
leadingCases.significance: Specific pregnancy risk assessment is mandatory — general assessment insufficient.

leadingCases.name: Kennedy v Cordia (Services) LLP [2016] UKSC 6
leadingCases.heldText: Inadequate risk assessment is strong evidence of breach of the employer's duty of care.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court — risk assessment adequacy as evidence of compliance.
